Glossary About
New syntheses · 15th – 17th c.

Renaissance & Late Scholasticism

The Renaissance inherits late scholastic psychology but opens it to humanism, Platonism, natural magic, and renewed spiritual introspection. Mind is reimagined as a site of ascent, creativity, contemplation, and inward reform.

1433 – 1499

Marsilio Ficino

The Platonic theologian who made the immortal soul a renaissance theme.

1462 – 1525

Pietro Pomponazzi

An Aristotelian pressing the mortality of the soul to the edge of orthodoxy.

1548 – 1617

Francisco Suárez

The late scholastic synthesis: soul, intellect, and substance on the threshold of modernity.

1515 – 1582

Teresa of Ávila

Interior prayer, self-knowledge, and the soul’s ascent through inward castles.

1401 – 1464

Nicholas of Cusa

Nicholas of Cusa can yield a genuine philosophy of mind, but only if we resist two reductions.

1542 – 1591

John of the Cross

Dark night, contemplation, and the soul’s ascent beyond images.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
